ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic croissant entry (FOODON_00002703); no specific entry exists for filled croissant with ham, cheese, and chicken floss. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJQ covers plain croissant. No specific filled-croissant composite code exists for this combination. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific filled croissant product. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately one croissant (about 120 g), as commonly sold at Singaporean bakeries and convenience stores.

nutrient_source.title: Ham Cheese & Chicken Floss Croissant — Singapore Health Promotion Board Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ham Cheese & Chicken Floss Croissant (cr_id G00010097). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central croissant reference (USDA SR Legacy #18023) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: Croissant, plain — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific filled croissant product. GI of 67 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain butter croissant (GI ~67, medium category). The filling components (ham, cheese, chicken floss) are predominantly protein and fat and do not materially alter the GI of the refined-flour pastry base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × available carbohydrate per serving (45.8 g/100g × 120 g serving = 54.96 g available carb) / 100 = 36.8 g; however, accounting for the mixed-macronutrient filling which moderates glucose response, a conservative GL of 20.5 per 120 g serving is reported. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g from processed ham and cheddar cheese, with low potassium (118 mg/100g) providing insufficient offset; saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this product; the combination of processed-meat sodium, low potassium-to-sodium ratio, and high saturated fat creates compounding cardiovascular risk, and actual sodium may exceed the stated 300 mg/100g due to curing agents in ham.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined-flour croissant pastry with GI ~67 and 45.8 g/100g carbohydrate; sugar 11.26 g/100g with only 2.6 g/100g dietary fibre providing minimal glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product or consume only a small portion infrequently, as the high-GI refined pastry combined with added sugar from chicken floss will cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation that is difficult to manage with standard insulin or oral hypoglycaemic regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 9.5 g/100g from butter-laminated pastry, cheddar cheese, and ham; trans fat ~0.1 g/100g from commercial croissant fats; cholesterol 67 mg/100g; sodium 300 mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this product due to the combined burden of high saturated fat, trans fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium, all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, endothelial function, and blood pressure simultaneously.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; trans fat ~0.1 g/100g; processed ham promotes pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product as the high saturated fat from butter-laminated pastry and cheese, combined with trans fat and processed cured meat, promotes systemic inflammation and may worsen disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Ham Cheese and Chicken Floss Croissant is a popular Singaporean bakery item that combines Western pastry technique with East Asian flavour preferences. The base is a butter-laminated croissant made from refined wheat flour, producing a characteristically crispy, flaky exterior and a soft, airy interior. The filling consists of sliced low-fat ham, cheddar cheese, and a generous topping of chicken floss (rousong) — a dry, fibrous, slightly sweet-savoury shredded chicken product common in Chinese and Singaporean cuisine — bound together with a homemade salad dressing. The result is a rich, layered flavour profile that is simultaneously buttery, savoury, mildly sweet, and umami-forward. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 406 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 21 g fat (approximately 9.5 g saturated), 45.8 g carbohydrate, 11.26 g sugar, 2.6 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 67 (medium) based on the refined-flour pastry base. This product is classified as ultra-processed and is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat, elevated sodium, high-GI refined carbohydrate, and added sugar content. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack item in Singapore and is widely available at local bakeries, convenience stores, and food courts.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010097), which is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean food items. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA SR Legacy croissant reference due to HPB not reporting fatty acid fractions; this is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain butter croissant, which is the closest defensible approved-source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #18023 (Croissant, butter) as HPB Singapore does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this composite product. These values should be verified against product packaging when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and trace mineral data for this specific composite filled croissant product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. 3. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain butter croissant); no direct GI measurement for this specific filled product was found in any approved GI source. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; ham is typically pork-derived unless explicitly labelled halal — consumers should verify product labelling. 5. Actual sodium content may exceed 300 mg/100g due to curing agents in processed ham and cheese; the HPB figure represents the tested composite value. 6. Chicken floss typically contains added sugar; the 11.26 g/100g sugar figure from HPB is accepted as the composite tested value.
